content:
# Luke Chapter 11 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 101-102 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_011_page_001.jpg, LUK_011_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And it came to pass, as he was in a certain place praying, when he ceased, one of his disciples said to him, "Lord, teach us to pray, just as John also taught his disciples."

**2** And he said to them, "When you pray, say: 'Father, hallowed be your name. Your kingdom come.

**3** Give us each day our daily bread.

**4** And forgive us our sins, for we ourselves also forgive everyone who is indebted to us. And lead us not into testing.'"

**5** And he said to them, "Which of you shall have a friend, and shall go to him at midnight and say to him, 'Friend, lend me three loaves,

**6** since a friend of mine has come to me from a journey, and I have nothing to set before him';

**7** and that one from within shall answer and say, 'Do not cause me trouble; the door has already been shut, and my children are with me in bed; I am not able to rise and give to you'?

**8** I say to you, even if he will not rise and give to him because he is his friend, yet because of his shamelessness he will rise and give him as many as he needs.

**9** And I say to you, ask, and it shall be given to you; seek, and you shall find; knock, and it shall be opened to you.

**10** For everyone who asks receives, and the one who seeks finds, and to the one who knocks it shall be opened.

**11** And what father among you, if his son shall ask for a fish, will instead of a fish give him a serpent?

**12** Or if he shall ask for an egg, will give him a scorpion?

**13** If you then, being evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more shall the Father from heaven give the Holy Spirit to those who ask him?"

**14** And he was casting out a demon, and it was mute. And it came to pass, when the demon had gone out, the mute man spoke; and the crowds marveled.

**15** But some of them said, "By Beelzebul, the ruler of the demons, he casts out the demons."

**16** And others, testing him, were seeking from him a sign from heaven.

**17** But he, knowing their thoughts, said to them, "Every kingdom divided against itself is brought to desolation, and a house divided against a house falls.

**18** And if Satan also is divided against himself, how shall his kingdom stand? Because you say that I cast out the demons by Beelzebul.

**19** But if I by Beelzebul cast out the demons, by whom do your sons cast them out? Therefore they shall be your judges.

**20** But if by the finger of God I cast out the demons, then the kingdom of God has come upon you.

**21** When the strong man, fully armed, guards his own court, his possessions are in peace.

**22** But when one stronger than he comes upon him and overcomes him, he takes away his full armor in which he had trusted, and distributes his spoils.

**23** The one who is not with me is against me, and the one who does not gather with me scatters.

**24** When the unclean spirit has gone out of the man, it passes through waterless places seeking rest; and finding none, it says, 'I will return to my house from which I came out.'

**25** And having come, it finds it swept and put in order.

**26** Then it goes and takes along seven other spirits more evil than itself, and having entered, they dwell there; and the last state of that man becomes worse than the first."

**27** And it came to pass, as he was saying these things, a certain woman from the crowd, having lifted up her voice, said to him, "Blessed is the womb that bore you and the breasts at which you nursed."

**28** But he said, "Rather, blessed are those who hear the word of God and keep it."

**29** And as the crowds were gathered together, he began to say, "This generation is an evil generation; it seeks a sign, and no sign shall be given to it except the sign of Jonah.

**30** For just as Jonah became a sign to the Ninevites, so also shall the Son of Man be to this generation.

**31** The queen of the south shall rise up in the judgment with the men of this generation and shall condemn them, for she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon; and behold, something greater than Solomon is here.

**32** The men of Nineveh shall stand up in the judgment with this generation and shall condemn it, for they repented at the preaching of Jonah; and behold, something greater than Jonah is here.

**33** No one, having lit a lamp, puts it in a hidden place nor under the bushel, but upon the lampstand, that those who enter may see the light.

**34** The lamp of the body is your eye. When your eye is sound, your whole body also is full of light; but when it is evil, your body also is full of darkness.

**35** Watch therefore, lest the light that is in you be darkness.

**36** If therefore your whole body is full of light, having no part dark, it shall be wholly full of light, as when the lamp with its brightness gives you light."

**37** And as he was speaking, a Pharisee asked him to dine with him; and having entered, he reclined at table.

**38** But the Pharisee, having seen it, marveled that he had not first washed before the meal.

**39** But the Lord said to him, "Now you Pharisees cleanse the outside of the cup and of the dish, but the inside of you is full of plunder and wickedness.

**40** Fools! Did not the one who made the outside also make the inside?

**41** But give as alms the things that are within, and behold, all things are clean to you.

**42** But woe to you Pharisees! For you tithe mint and rue and every herb, and you pass by justice and the love of God; but these things it was needful to do, and those not to leave undone.

**43** Woe to you Pharisees! For you love the chief seat in the synagogues and the greetings in the marketplaces.

**44** Woe to you! For you are as the unseen tombs, and the men who walk over them do not know it."

**45** And one of the lawyers, answering, said to him, "Teacher, in saying these things you insult us also."

**46** And he said, "Woe to you lawyers also! For you load men with burdens hard to bear, and you yourselves do not touch the burdens with one of your fingers.

**47** Woe to you! For you build the tombs of the prophets, and your fathers killed them.

**48** Therefore you are witnesses and consent to the works of your fathers; for they indeed killed them, and you build their tombs.

**49** On account of this also the Wisdom of God said, 'I will send to them prophets and apostles, and some of them they shall kill and persecute,'

**50** that the blood of all the prophets, which has been shed from the foundation of the world, may be required of this generation,

**51** from the blood of Abel to the blood of Zechariah, who perished between the altar and the house. Yes, I say to you, it shall be required of this generation.

**52** Woe to you lawyers! For you have taken away the key of knowledge; you yourselves did not enter, and those who were entering you hindered."

**53** And when he had gone out from there, the scribes and the Pharisees began to press upon him vehemently and to provoke him to speak about many things,

**54** lying in wait for him, to catch something out of his mouth.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Verification

The two Sinaiticus manuscript images (pages 101-102) were examined. Page 1 covers approximately Luke 11:1 through Luke 11:28 across four columns. Page 2 covers approximately Luke 11:33 through Luke 12:1 across four columns. The text is in Sinaiticus's characteristic continuous uncial script, four columns per page. Key readings confirmed from the manuscript:

- ΠΑΤΕΡ (11:2, "Father" — bare vocative without "our" or "in the heavens"; shorter Lukan Lord's Prayer)
- ΤΟΝ ΑΡΤΟΝ ΗΜΩΝ ΤΟΝ ΕΠΙΟΥΣΙΟΝ (11:3, "our daily bread" — the debated *epiousios*)
- ΑΜΑΡΤΙΑΣ (11:4, "sins" — not "debts" as in Matthew's parallel)
- ΠΕΙΡΑΣΜΟΝ (11:4, "testing" — without the concluding "but deliver us from evil")
- ΑΝΑΙΔΕΙΑΝ (11:8, "shamelessness" — NT hapax legomenon)
- Fish/serpent and egg/scorpion pairs only (11:11-12 — Sinaiticus omits the bread/stone pair)
- Ο ΠΑΤΗΡ Ο ΕΞ ΟΥΡΑΝΟΥ (11:13, "the Father from heaven" — unusual construction)
- ΠΝΕΥΜΑ ΑΓΙΟΝ (11:13, "Holy Spirit" — distinctively Lukan, where Matthew reads "good things")
- ΒΕΕΛΖΕΒΟΥΛ (11:15, 18, 19 — consistent spelling throughout)
- ΔΙΑΝΟΗΜΑΤΑ (11:17, "thoughts/deliberations" — rare word)
- ΔΑΚΤΥΛΩ ΘΕΟΥ (11:20, "finger of God" — Exodus 8:19 allusion; Matthew reads "Spirit of God")
- ΚΑΘΩΠΛΙΣΜΕΝΟΣ (11:21, "fully armed" — complete military equipment)
- ΠΑΝΟΠΛΙΑΝ (11:22, "full armor" — NT usage also in Ephesians 6:11, 13)
- ΕΠΑΡΑΣΑ ΦΩΝΗΝ (11:27, "having lifted up a voice" — Semitic idiom)
- ΣΗΜΕΙΟΝ ΙΩΝΑ (11:29, "sign of Jonah" — without Matthew's explicit three-day interpretation)
- ΠΛΕΙΟΝ (11:31, 32 — neuter "something greater," not masculine "someone greater")
- ΚΡΥΠΤΗΝ (11:33, "hidden place/cellar" — Lukan addition to lamp saying)
- ΑΠΛΟΥΣ (11:34, "single/sound" — with overtones of generosity)
- ΑΡΠΑΓΗΣ ΚΑΙ ΠΟΝΗΡΙΑΣ (11:39, "plunder and wickedness" — Luke's pairing)
- ΤΑ ΕΝΟΝΤΑ ΔΟΤΕ ΕΛΕΗΜΟΣΥΝΗΝ (11:41, "give the things within as alms" — notoriously difficult)
- ΠΗΓΑΝΟΝ (11:42, "rue" — where Matthew reads "dill")
- ΤΑ ΜΝΗΜΕΙΑ ΤΑ ΑΔΗΛΑ (11:44, "unseen/unmarked tombs")
- ΝΟΜΙΚΩΝ (11:45, 46, 52, "lawyers" — distinguished from Pharisees)
- Η ΣΟΦΙΑ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ ΕΙΠΕΝ (11:49, "the Wisdom of God said" — unique quotation formula)
- ΤΟΥ ΟΙΚΟΥ (11:51, "the house" — not "the temple" as in some manuscripts)
- ΚΛΕΙΔΑ ΤΗΣ ΓΝΩΣΕΩΣ (11:52, "key of knowledge")
- ΔΕΙΝΩΣ ΕΝΕΧΕΙΝ (11:53, "press upon vehemently" — intense hostile pressure)
- ΕΝΕΔΡΕΥΟΝΤΕΣ (11:54, "lying in wait" — hunting/ambush language)

### Translation Principles Applied

1. **11:2-4 — Sinaiticus shorter Lord's Prayer preserved:** The shorter Lukan form is followed without harmonization toward Matthew. The bare vocative "Father" without "our" or "who art in heaven" is retained. The absent petitions ("your will be done" and "deliver us from evil") are not supplied. All three translators agreed on following the Sinaiticus text.

2. **11:4 — "testing" vs. "temptation":** EMET renders "testing," LOGOS "temptation," PNEUMA "a time of testing." SOPHIA follows EMET — *peirasmos* in this context denotes trial or testing rather than moral enticement; "testing" is more precise and avoids the narrower English connotation of "temptation."

3. **11:8 — "shamelessness" vs. "persistence":** EMET renders "shamelessness," LOGOS "persistence," PNEUMA "bold persistence." SOPHIA follows EMET — *anaideia* literally means "shamelessness/lack of shame," not persistence per se. The word's literal force should be preserved, allowing readers to encounter the striking Greek term. LOGOS and PNEUMA interpret toward application; SOPHIA preserves the lexical meaning.

4. **11:13 — "the Father from heaven":** EMET renders "the Father, the one out of heaven," LOGOS "the Father from heaven," PNEUMA "the Father in heaven." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — Sinaiticus reads *ho pater ho ex ouranou* ("the Father, the one from heaven"), not "in heaven" as PNEUMA renders. LOGOS captures the unusual *ex* construction concisely.

5. **11:17 — "divided" vs. "torn apart":** EMET renders "having been divided," LOGOS "divided," PNEUMA "torn apart by civil war." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "divided" is the precise formal equivalent; PNEUMA's expansion goes beyond the Greek.

6. **11:21 — "court" vs. "courtyard" vs. "estate":** EMET renders "courtyard," LOGOS "court," PNEUMA "estate." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "court" for *aulen* is the concise formal equivalent; "estate" (PNEUMA) expands beyond the term.

7. **11:27 — "bore" vs. "carried" vs. "gave birth to":** EMET renders "having carried," LOGOS "that bore," PNEUMA "who gave you birth." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "bore" is the natural English rendering for *bastasasa* in this context (carrying a child in the womb).

8. **11:29 — "gathered together" vs. "increasing":** EMET renders "being gathered together," LOGOS "were increasing," PNEUMA "were increasing." SOPHIA follows EMET's sense — Sinaiticus reads *epathroizomenon* ("being gathered together/crowding upon"), not *epauaxanomenon*; "gathered together" more accurately reflects the manuscript.

9. **11:33 — "bushel" vs. "measuring-basket" vs. "basket":** EMET renders "measuring-basket," LOGOS "bushel," PNEUMA "basket." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "bushel" is the established traditional rendering of *modion*; precise enough without being overly technical.

10. **11:34 — "sound" vs. "single" vs. "healthy":** EMET renders "single," LOGOS "sound," PNEUMA "healthy." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "sound" captures the sense of *haplous* (clear, whole, undivided) better than the overly literal "single" (EMET) or the medical-sounding "healthy" (PNEUMA).

11. **11:38 — "washed" vs. "baptized":** EMET renders "baptized" (literal for *ebaptisthe*), LOGOS "washed," PNEUMA "perform the ritual washing." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "washed" correctly conveys the ritual hand-washing practice without confusion with sacramental baptism, while remaining close to the Greek.

12. **11:39 — "But" vs. "And":** EMET renders "And the Lord said," LOGOS "But the Lord said." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — the adversative "But" captures the contrast between the Pharisee's expectation and Jesus' response, consistent with SOPHIA's approach in Luke 10:41.

13. **11:40 — "Fools" vs. "Senseless ones" vs. "How foolish":** EMET renders "Senseless ones," LOGOS "Fools," PNEUMA "How foolish you are." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "Fools" is the direct, forceful rendering of *aphrones*; EMET's compound is unnecessary, PNEUMA's exclamation dilutes the vocative address.

14. **11:42 — "needful to do" vs. "necessary to do":** EMET renders "necessary," LOGOS "needful." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "needful" is the slightly more elevated register appropriate to Jesus' authoritative pronouncement, consistent with SOPHIA's rendering in 10:42.

15. **11:42 — "leave undone" vs. "pass by":** EMET and LOGOS both use "pass by" for the first clause but differ on the final phrase. EMET renders "not to pass by," LOGOS "not to leave undone." SOPHIA follows LOGOS for the final clause — "leave undone" avoids the repetition of "pass by" within the same verse and captures the sense of *pareinai* (to neglect, leave aside).

16. **11:44 — "unseen tombs" vs. "unmarked graves":** EMET renders "unseen tombs," LOGOS "unseen tombs," PNEUMA "unmarked graves." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "unseen tombs" preserves *adela* ("unseen/not evident") more precisely than "unmarked."

17. **11:45 — "lawyers" vs. "law-experts" vs. "legal experts":** EMET renders "law-experts," LOGOS "lawyers," PNEUMA "legal experts." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "lawyers" is the established formal equivalent for *nomikoi*, consistent with SOPHIA's decision in Luke 10:25.

18. **11:46 — "men" vs. "people":** EMET renders "the men," LOGOS "men," PNEUMA "people." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — *tous anthropous* in this context is rendered "men" as the formal equivalent, without the definite article (which is generic in Greek).

19. **11:51 — "the house" vs. "the sanctuary":** EMET and LOGOS render "the house," PNEUMA "the sanctuary." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — Sinaiticus reads *tou oikou*, not *tou naou*; "the house" preserves the manuscript reading precisely.

20. **11:53 — "press upon him vehemently" vs. "be terribly hostile" vs. "oppose him fiercely":** EMET renders "be terribly hostile," LOGOS "press upon him vehemently," PNEUMA "oppose him fiercely." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "press upon vehemently" captures *deinōs enechein* (literally "terribly hold against/press") with precision; EMET's "terribly hostile" and PNEUMA's "oppose fiercely" are interpretive.

21. **11:54 — "lying in wait" vs. "lying in ambush" vs. "plotting":** EMET renders "lying in ambush," LOGOS "lying in wait," PNEUMA "plotting." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "lying in wait" renders *enedreuontes* accurately; "plotting" (PNEUMA) loses the ambush imagery.

22. **11:54 — "catch something out of his mouth" vs. "catch something from his mouth":** EMET renders "from his mouth," LOGOS "out of his mouth." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "out of" preserves the Greek *ek* more precisely.

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA, from Codex Sinaiticus (4th century CE).*
